<|9.96|> Almost always in that moment, what is remembered or recalled is really poignant and so lovely<|18.22|>
<|18.22|> and so specific.<|19.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the grand scheme of time, and especially within what we now know of as evolutionary history,<|9.04|>
<|9.90|> these stories and memories that are so heartfelt and tender, these artifacts of the heart,<|17.32|>
<|18.66|> are tiny, just tiny, tiny.<|22.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And with the overall fabric of time and space, they can even appear insignificant in light of everything else.<|8.94|>
<|9.78|> There's humility in such a realization if it helps us to become aware of what a small place we have within space and time.<|18.92|>
<|19.44|> That realization can also be really deadly if it leads to despair.<|24.42|>
<|25.42|> Simply asking then what matters?<|27.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.52|> These three readings offer us a complimentary and I think much happier perspective on how tiny our lives sometimes feel.<|11.46|>
<|13.04|> The realization is this, that nothing of good is lost in Christ.<|21.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.02|> Nothing of the good, nothing of truth, nothing of beauty is lost in Christ.<|7.20|>
<|7.56|> Yes, our lives feel like an elusive dream.<|10.94|>
<|11.78|> But in light of these passages, and especially the comfort of Thessalonians,<|15.72|>
<|16.82|> God remembers all of these dreams and all the people who dream them.<|23.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Faith, then, is in part simply this, trusting that God's memory is better than ours.<|11.18|>
<|12.94|> And salvation, in part, is also related to the divine memory, right?<|20.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For whatever and whoever God remembers is safe and sound, whole and healed in the divine mind.<|12.38|><|endoftext|>
